metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel filename is not canonicalized in any child. S5d defines the behavior ('sentinel file in the state volume, presence = stop') but never names the file. S5a lists 'the emergency-stop sentinel (S5d)' as a state-volume resident but also does not name it. Operators must know the exact filename to create/remove it during an incident; an undocumented filename defeats the incident-control purpose.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) AC 'Emergency-stop' + child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) AC 'State volume'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5d's emergency-stop AC: 'The sentinel filename is emergency_stop (no extension) at the root of the state volume.' Mirror this in S5a's state-volume AC list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the status endpoint port publication (e.g. `ports: ["8080:8080"]`) to S5a's docker-compose.yml edit and AC test, and lock the port number as a shared contract between S5a and S5c.
findings.finding: S5a does not mention publishing the autolander status endpoint port in the compose service definition, yet S5c's nginx route and `land`'s `lander_down` check depend on that port being reachable. No dependency is declared from S5c to S5a for this port contract.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) — infra/compose/docker-compose.yml autolander service ports; child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — AC1 status endpoint
findings.suggested_fix: In S5a's AC and test list, add: (a) a named port (e.g. 8080) published by the autolander compose service, and (b) a check in `docker compose config` validation that the ports stanza is present. In S5c's AC, reference 'the port published by S5a (8080)' rather than 'e.g. :8080'. Declare a formal dep from S5c to S5a.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a hang simulation test to S5d (or S5a) that freezes the bot's poll loop, asserts the HEALTHCHECK transitions to unhealthy after the staleness threshold, and asserts autoheal issues a docker restart — completing the end-to-end liveness proof.
findings.finding: S5a states 'the restart behaviour itself is exercised by S5d's hang test' but S5d's crash-recovery test suite covers only kill-at-phase and drain⇄autoheal-race scenarios — there is no hang simulation (heartbeat goes stale → HEALTHCHECK marks unhealthy → autoheal issues docker restart) in any child's test list.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) — AC autoheal sidecar wired; child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — AC crash-recovery/idempotency test
findings.suggested_fix: In S5d's test list, add: 'hang simulation: suspend the poll loop (e.g. SIGSTOP the process or mock the heartbeat writer to stop writing), wait for HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold + autoheal polling interval, assert container transitions to unhealthy and is restarted by autoheal, assert no double-submit or stranded stack after restart.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S5c's AC3 and test: the monitoring_autolander.tf alarm threshold is explicitly set to > 2700 s (45 min) and the unit test asserts this threshold value in the alarm expression.
findings.finding: S5c's AC does not pin the design-fixed 45-minute stuck-state alarm threshold mandated by the parent, leaving the threshold unverifiable from S5c's AC alone and risking a wrong value being configured in monitoring_autolander.tf.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — AC3 stuck-state alarm; parent design context 'Stuck-state threshold (design-fixed)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c AC3, add: 'The alarm threshold is set to 2700 s (45 min, the design-fixed value); the unit test asserts the `threshold` field in the CloudWatch alarm resource equals 2700.' In the tf resource: `threshold = 2700` with a comment referencing the design-fixed contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the heartbeat file explicitly (e.g. `heartbeat.ts`) in both S5d's AC1 and S5c's AC1, and reference the same name in S5a's state volume AC so all three children share a canonical path.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file's name/path is never specified in any child's AC — S5d calls it 'a timestamp file in the state volume' and S5c says it reads its age for heartbeat_age_s, but unlike autolander.lock (explicitly named), the heartbeat filename is an implicit cross-child contract with no canonical name in either AC.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — AC1 heartbeat; child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) — AC1 heartbeat_age_s
findings.suggested_fix: Pick a canonical filename (e.g. `autolander.heartbeat`) and add it explicitly to S5d AC1 ('writes /state/autolander.heartbeat'), S5c AC1 ('reads /state/autolander.heartbeat for heartbeat_age_s'), and S5a AC3 (list it by name alongside autolander.lock).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `phase=submitting + re_drive_count >= N` row to the S5d restart-reconciliation decision table, specifying the fallback (e.g., record a `ci_failed`/`needs_rebase` outcome via S3's hand-back protocol, emit `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`, and stop driving the stack).
findings.finding: The S5d restart-reconciliation decision table includes `re_drive_count` in the `recovery.json` schema and specifies that `phase=submitting` on restart triggers a re-drive when not all members are MERGED, but it provides no fallback when `re_drive_count` has already reached the S2b N=5 re-drive bound. The decision rule is incomplete: the table has no row or clause for 'submitting + re_drive_count >= 5'.
findings.location: S5d AC (Restart reconciliation, phase=submitting rule) + S2b (N=5 re-drive bound)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct S5b's title to remove 'All-Projects capability' — replace with 'rebar-project Rebase ACL' or equivalent, matching the AC's explicit scope.
findings.finding: S5b's title 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning' contains 'All-Projects capability' as a residual reference — the AC explicitly states the rebase right is NOT on All-Projects and NOT a global capability, but on the rebar project's refs/meta/config. This title-content contradiction could cause a developer to mistakenly configure an All-Projects-level permission.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — title vs. AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Rename S5b to: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + rebar-project Rebase ACL provisioning' to match what AC3 actually specifies.

findings.finding: S5b's title reads 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning' but the child body and every AC explicitly contradict 'All-Projects capability': the Rebase grant is on the rebar project's refs/meta/config (NOT All-Projects), there is NO global Gerrit [capability] grant, and the title phrase 'submit ACL provisioning' is also misleading since the AC explicitly states 'do NOT add a submit grant.' This residual title text directly contradicts the child's own normative content.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — title and AC 'Rebase-on-behalf access right'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + Rebase access right (rebar project)' — dropping both 'All-Projects capability' (contradicted by body) and 'submit ACL provisioning' (explicitly excluded by body).

findings.finding: S5b's ticket title reads 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + All-Projects capability + submit ACL provisioning' but the description and all ACs explicitly state the grant goes to the rebar project's own refs/meta/config — NOT All-Projects, NOT a global capability. 'All-Projects capability' in the title is a stale residual reference that directly contradicts the implementation intent.
findings.location: child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — ticket title vs. AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Rename S5b to: 'S5b Credentials (SSM) + Rebase access right on rebar project refs/heads/*'.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The new CloudWatch alarms in monitoring_autolander.tf (NEW) are never specified to wire alarm_actions to the shared aws_sns_topic.alerts, so they could be created as silent alarms — directly contradicting the story's 'LOUD failures' and 'highly visible' reliability posture.
findings.location: S5c AC: 'Error metric (LOUD by default)' / 'Alarm in monitoring_autolander.tf' / S5 parent plan: 'fail LOUDLY'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the error-metric alarm and the stuck-state alarm, add: 'alarm_actions and ok_actions reference aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn (the shared alert sink declared in monitoring.tf), consistent with the repo alarm convention.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete, authoritative `phase` enum vocabulary (at minimum: `idle`, `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, `paused`) in one co-located place (e.g., a table in S5d or a shared schema section) and cross-reference it from the status endpoint schema (S5c), the alarm threshold (Design context), and the restart-reconciliation decision table (S5d).
findings.finding: The `phase` field is used as an enum across at least four locations (recovery.json schema in S5d, wipChain status-endpoint JSON in S5c, the stuck-state alarm threshold in the parent plan, and the emergency-stop AC in S5d), but its authoritative full value vocabulary is never defined in a single co-located place. The decision table in S5d names only `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`; the parent plan adds `idle` (for the 'non-idle phase' alarm condition); S5d AC adds `paused` (emergency-stop transition). An implementor writing the status endpoint, the HEALTHCHECK, or the alarm expression must infer the complete set by reading all four locations — and may miss values.
findings.location: S5d AC (restart reconciliation table) + S5c AC (status endpoint) + Design context (stuck-state alarm, 'non-idle phase') + S5d AC (emergency-stop, 'paused')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Phase enum (authoritative)' table to S5d listing all values with their semantics: `idle` (no wipChain), `rebasing`, `awaiting_verified`, `submitting`, `paused` (emergency-stop active). Reference this table explicitly in S5c's status endpoint shape and in the stuck-state alarm expression. Extend the S5d restart-reconciliation table with a `phase=paused` row (suggested rule: treat same as `rebasing` — discard and re-select when sentinel is absent, or stay paused if sentinel still present).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback procedure in S5 (e.g., in a runbook or README): (1) activate emergency-stop sentinel or `docker-compose stop autolander`; (2) assert that all changes remain in their Gerrit queued state (no data cleanup needed); (3) steps to re-enable after fix.
findings.finding: The plan does not contain an explicit rollback procedure describing how to quickly revert to manual landing if the deployed service misbehaves, nor does it state that in-flight Gerrit changes are safe (no data cleanup needed) after a rollback.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5d (liveness + crash-safe recovery); Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out procedure for the compose deployment (e.g., 'remove the autolander service block, run `docker compose up -d` to restore prior state; revoke Gerrit `Rebase` access right') so operators have a concrete recovery path if the deployment destabilizes the Gerrit box.
findings.finding: The plan deploys a new docker-compose service onto the live Gerrit production box (modifying the shared compose stack), but provides no rollback or back-out procedure if the deployment goes wrong — e.g., if the new container fails to start, consumes unexpected resources, or the compose file change adversely affects adjacent services. The Docker-socket RW mount is justified but also lacks a back-out path. Acknowledgement of the approach is present, but the reversibility mechanism is absent.
findings.location: Scope section; AC bullet S5a ('Dockerfile + compose service + autoheal sidecar'); Design context 'Autoheal socket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to S5a (or the parent story) stating: (1) remove the autolander stanza from the compose file and re-run `docker compose up -d` to restore the prior compose state; (2) delete the SSM token if partially provisioned; (3) revoke the `Rebase` access right from the bot account. Reference this procedure from the S5a AC so it is checked during sign-off.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-deploy measurement step (e.g., 'after one week of production data, review CI p95 duration and heartbeat jitter logs, and adjust `STUCK_PHASE_THRESHOLD_S` and `HEARTBEAT_LANDER_DOWN_S` if needed') so the asserted thresholds are validated against real behavior rather than left as permanent assumptions.
findings.finding: The stuck-state threshold (45 min = '30-min CI-await bound + margin') and the heartbeat thresholds (15 s write, 90 s lander_down) are asserted by reasoning rather than derived from measurement, and no post-deploy tuning/measurement plan is stated. Both are described as 'design-fixed' or 'configurable' but neither has a stated plan to validate against observed CI durations or poll jitter after initial deployment.
findings.location: Design context: 'Stuck-state threshold (design-fixed)' and 'Heartbeat contract (design-fixed)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c or S5d, add a tuning note: instrument the actual CI-await durations in the first week via the `autolander_time_to_land_seconds` metric (already planned), set an explicit review gate at day-7 to confirm the 45-min threshold has < 1% false-positive rate, and lower or raise it accordingly. For heartbeat, log each write-to-read latency and alert if p99 > 30 s before the 90 s threshold trips.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a staged-activation mechanism (e.g., a `DRY_RUN=true` env flag that logs intended submissions without executing them, or a per-project allowlist) so the service can be validated in production before it acts on all changes.
findings.finding: The plan deploys a net-new auto-submitter that immediately acts on all qualifying Gerrit changes upon `compose up`, with no staged rollout, dry-run mode, or per-project opt-in flag to limit initial blast radius.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a (compose service); 'What' section; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add `AUTOLANDER_DRY_RUN` env var (default `true` in initial deploy, set to `false` to go live) to S5a/S5b scope; in dry-run mode the loop logs 'would submit' but calls no Gerrit mutating APIs. Document the activation sequence: deploy with dry-run=true → validate logs/metrics for one cycle → flip to false.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `willfarrell/autoheal` publishes an arm64 manifest (e.g. `docker manifest inspect willfarrell/autoheal | jq '.[].platform'`) and pin the image to a specific digest/tag that includes arm64; document this in S5a's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The plan selects `willfarrell/autoheal` as the restart sidecar but does not verify that a multi-arch (arm64) image is available for it. The Gerrit box is an AL2023 arm64/t4g instance; the entire existing compose stack — Dockerfile.reviewbot, docker-compose.yml comments, gerrit image selection — is explicitly arm64-aware. An autoheal image that publishes only amd64 would silently pull the wrong arch or fail to start on this host.
findings.location: Design context / S5a child task (08e1-5505-d898-4192) — 'willfarrell/autoheal sidecar' in docker-compose.yml edit
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5a AC: 'The `willfarrell/autoheal` image tag used in docker-compose.yml is confirmed multi-arch (linux/arm64) via `docker manifest inspect <image:tag>`; pin to a specific version tag (not `latest`) and record the digest. If arm64 is absent, substitute an arm64-compatible alternative (e.g. `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal` or a minimal custom restart sidecar).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `willfarrell/autoheal` publishes a `linux/arm64` manifest (probe `docker manifest inspect willfarrell/autoheal:latest` or the Docker Hub tags page), confirm the `autoheal=true` label name is correct for the chosen version, and pin an explicit image tag (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.1`) in `docker-compose.yml` so a breaking upstream change cannot silently alter behavior.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new Docker sidecar (not present anywhere in the existing compose stack) and asserts a specific label contract — `autoheal=true` on the container triggers restart on `unhealthy` — but neither the parent plan nor any child ticket verifies this label name, the image's arm64 availability, or pins an image tag. The Gerrit box is an AL2023 arm64/t4g instance; if `willfarrell/autoheal` does not publish a linux/arm64 manifest, the sidecar will silently run under QEMU emulation or fail to pull entirely, defeating the autoheal posture the whole liveness story depends on.
findings.location: Design context / S5a AC (autoheal sidecar) / S5d AC4 (HEALTHCHECK + autoheal)
findings.suggested_fix: In docker-compose.yml use `image: willfarrell/autoheal:<pinned-tag>` and add a comment confirming arm64 multi-arch support was verified at that tag. If arm64 is not available, evaluate `ghcr.io/willfarrell/autoheal` or an alternative sidecar (e.g. a custom HEALTHCHECK-triggered restart via a host systemd timer).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit dependency from S5c to S5d (or vice versa) for loop.py edits, OR combine the loop.py work into a single child, OR add an integration note specifying which child lands first and what the other may assume is present.
findings.finding: S5c and S5d both edit `infra/autolander/loop.py` with no declared dependency between them and no stated merge or integration plan, creating an ordering gap and merge-conflict risk for a large, interleaved set of additions.
findings.location: child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) and child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) — file infra/autolander/loop.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a formal depends_on from S5c to S5d (S5d owns heartbeat, which S5c's endpoint reads) so S5d lands first and S5c's endpoint implementation can reference the heartbeat writer. Alternatively, carve loop.py into two non-overlapping sections with a clear interface (e.g., S5d writes the heartbeat file; S5c reads it by agreed filename) and document the agreed filename in both children's ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S5a's AC and docker compose config test: the autolander service declares env_file referencing the shared .env (or equivalent environment: injection), and the test asserts AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN is visible inside the container.
findings.finding: No child verifies that the autolander compose service passes the AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN env-var (materialized by S5b's fetch-secrets.sh) into the container. S5a's AC and tests do not include this env-var wiring, and no dependency is declared from S5a or S5d to S5b.
findings.location: child 08e1-5505-d898-4192 (S5a) — infra/compose/docker-compose.yml; child ea61-01c3-daea-4824 (S5b) — fetch-secrets.sh
findings.suggested_fix: In S5a's docker-compose.yml edit, add `env_file: - /path/to/shared/.env` (or `environment: - AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`) to the autolander service stanza. Add to S5a's `docker compose config` test: assert AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN is present in the autolander service environment. Declare a dep from S5a to S5b.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `SITE_SUBDIRS` in the plan (state its type, source, and default value) so the S5a executor can implement `compose-up.sh` without clarification.
findings.finding: The term 'SITE_SUBDIRS' appears in the S5a AC ('compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS') but is never defined or explained in the plan. An executor has no basis to know what value(s) to supply, what the variable controls, or where it originates.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a bullet: '+ compose-up.sh SITE_SUBDIRS'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the S5a AC or Design context, e.g.: '`SITE_SUBDIRS` is a space-separated list of per-site state-volume subdirectories (e.g. `site-a site-b`); default is a single entry matching the Gerrit hostname.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable artifact for the 'recovery record' in the S5d AC (e.g., a named JSON file written to the state volume on graceful drain listing in-flight change IDs and phase).
findings.finding: The S5d AC references 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record' but does not specify what artifact or observable form the recovery record takes (e.g., a JSON file in the state volume, a structured log line, a Gerrit comment). 'Concrete' signals specificity is intended but leaves the observable form undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5d bullet: 'drain-on-SIGTERM with a concrete recovery record'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the AC to: 'drain-on-SIGTERM writes a `recovery.json` to the state volume listing in-flight wipChain IDs and their phases; on restart the loop reads and logs this file before resuming normal polling.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the S5c AC (or its Design context block), enumerate what the three feeders of `autolander_errors` are (e.g., loop exception handler, Gerrit API error, rebase/submit failure) so the story-level criterion is independently verifiable.
findings.finding: The S5c AC references '`autolander_errors` (three feeders + alarm)' but neither the plan body (What, Scope, Design context) nor any other section describes or enumerates what the 'three feeders' are; a story-level verifier cannot confirm this AC is met without that definition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5c bullet: '`autolander_errors` (three feeders + alarm)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the Design context or S5c AC, e.g.: 'three feeders: (1) unhandled loop exception, (2) Gerrit API call failure, (3) rebase/submit hard error'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the autoheal ownership split between S5a and S5d — e.g., add a note that S5a owns the sidecar container declaration in compose and S5d owns the HEALTHCHECK instruction and liveness-probe behavior that the sidecar acts on.
findings.finding: The term 'autoheal' appears in both the S5a AC ('autoheal sidecar') and the S5d AC ('HEALTHCHECK + autoheal') without the boundary between the two children being made explicit; an implementer could duplicate work or leave a gap in wiring the HEALTHCHECK to the sidecar.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S5a bullet and S5d bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the S5a AC, change 'autoheal sidecar' to 'autoheal sidecar container (compose declaration)'; in the S5d AC, change 'HEALTHCHECK + autoheal' to 'HEALTHCHECK instruction (triggers the S5a autoheal sidecar)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing the three novel decisions introduced by S5: (1) Docker socket write privilege for autoheal and why it is accepted, (2) the heartbeat liveness contract (`heartbeat_age_s` field, 15 s write / 90 s alarm) as a published interface between the bot and `land`, and (3) the status-endpoint contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — Docker socket write privilege for autoheal, a new cross-service heartbeat liveness contract (`heartbeat_age_s` bound to `land`'s `lander_down`), and a new read-only HTTP status endpoint contract — but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: Design context section; AC S5c (status endpoint); AC S5d (heartbeat, autoheal socket)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0001-autolander-deploy-contracts.md` (or add a section to an existing CLAUDE.md) with: the autoheal socket-write justification, the heartbeat threshold values as a versioned contract, and the status-endpoint field names. Reference this doc from the S5 design-context section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the autolander's read-only status endpoint is network-reachable from where `land` runs: add either (a) a published host port in the compose service definition + an nginx location block in `rebar.conf.template` (e.g. `location /autolander/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:<PORT>/; }`) or (b) an explicit internal-docker-network binding with a stated access contract, so the `heartbeat_age_s` → `lander_down` data-flow is mechanically complete.
findings.finding: The status endpoint's network reachability path is unspecified: the plan says the endpoint is 'bound so agents can reach it' and that land's lander_down binds to heartbeat_age_s from it, but neither the parent plan nor child S5c specifies a port binding in docker-compose.yml, a corresponding nginx location block, or any other network path. The existing nginx config (rebar.conf.template) only proxies /review/ and /, with no autolander route. Without a specified port or proxy path, the data-flow from the bot's status endpoint to land's lander_down check is mechanically incomplete.
findings.location: Design context ('Status model') + AC S5c ('Read-only status endpoint') + infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `ports: ["127.0.0.1:${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT:-8001}:8001"]` entry to the autolander compose service (parallel to review-bot's REVIEW_BOT_PORT pattern), single-source AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT from fetch-secrets.sh/.env, and add a `location /autolander/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:${AUTOLANDER_STATUS_PORT}/; }` block in rebar.conf.template — then reference that URL in the lander_down contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the status endpoint's binding address and human/admin access mechanism: either (a) bind to loopback only (`127.0.0.1:<port>`) with a note that remote access requires SSM port-forward, or (b) add a `/autolander/` nginx proxy location (with any required auth) and document it in S5c/S5a, or (c) state an explicit, justified no-auth rationale if access is intentionally open.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) never specifies how human operators and administrators authenticate to it, nor whether it is bound exclusively to loopback / proxied through the existing nginx TLS gateway, leaving its human/admin access contract unspecified for a host that has ports open to 0.0.0.0/0.
findings.location: S5c AC1 — 'Read-only status endpoint'; Design context — 'bound so agents can reach it'
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c AC1, add: 'The endpoint binds to `127.0.0.1:<STATUS_PORT>` (loopback only, never published directly to the internet); remote operator access is via SSM Session Manager port-forward. No nginx proxy block is required.' OR add a `/autolander/` location block to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` proxying to the loopback port, and document it in S5c.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name and verify the Gerrit 3.14.1 REST endpoint for rebase-on-behalf-of-uploader (likely `POST /a/changes/{id}/rebase` with `{"on_behalf_of_uploader": true}`) by checking the live instance's REST API documentation at `<host>/Documentation/rest-api-changes.html#rebase-change` or the swagger explorer, and add the confirmed endpoint path + body parameter to the S5b smoke-test step 4 so the rebase POST call is explicit (not just the verification GET).
findings.finding: S5b designs the bot's rebase-on-behalf-of-uploader capability around a Gerrit REST endpoint, but the specific endpoint surface (`POST /a/changes/{id}/rebase` with an `on_behalf_of_uploader` body parameter) is a new integration that does not appear anywhere in the codebase. The plan acknowledges the endpoint indirectly via a smoke-test curl in step 4, but never names the concrete REST path or the exact JSON body field. The smoke-test step 4 curl hits `a/changes/{id}/detail?o=CURRENT_REVISION` (a read) to verify uploader preservation — but does not show the actual rebase POST call the bot will issue, leaving the rebase endpoint and its parameter contract unverified.
findings.location: S5b AC (Rebase-on-behalf access right, concrete) / smoke-check-autolander.sh step 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5b: 'The rebase call is `POST /a/changes/{change-id}/rebase` with body `{"on_behalf_of_uploader": true}` (confirmed against Gerrit 3.14.1 REST docs at `<host>/Documentation/rest-api-changes.html#rebase-change`). The smoke-test step 4 issues this POST directly before asserting uploader preservation via the GET detail call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5c: Declare the status endpoint's access class — specify the binding interface (e.g., localhost-only vs. 0.0.0.0), port, whether it is internal-only (and how that is enforced, e.g., host firewall / VPN) or public-with-rationale, and whether any authentication is required, so the posture is explicit rather than implied.
findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) introduced in this plan is described as 'bound so agents can reach it' but its access posture is undeclared: the plan does not state whether the endpoint binds to localhost-only or 0.0.0.0, what port is used, whether it is accessible only from the internal Gerrit box network or from broader networks (including wherever `land` clients run), and whether it requires any authentication. The plan labels it 'non-secret' but that is a data characterisation, not an access-class declaration. Since `land` clients read `heartbeat_age_s` from it, the endpoint may need to be reachable from developer machines or CI runners outside the Gerrit host, widening its effective exposure beyond the local host.
findings.location: S5c AC1 (S5c child ticket, 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03, Acceptance Criteria #1); parent plan 'Observability + status endpoint' AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add an access-class declaration to the S5c AC1 description, e.g.: 'The endpoint binds to 127.0.0.1:<port> on the Gerrit host (internal-only; `land` clients reach it via SSH tunnel / internal VPC routing — no internet exposure). No authentication is required because the data is non-secret and the network boundary enforces isolation.' Alternatively, if broader binding is required, document the firewall rule that restricts access and the rationale for the public posture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete format of the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` structured stdout markers (e.g., a JSON line schema with required fields such as `{"marker": "AUTOLANDER_ERROR", "ts": "<ISO8601>", "phase": "...", "error": "..."}`) co-located in S5c, and specify the exact grep/parse key that `observability.sh` uses as the feeder trigger.
findings.finding: The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` and `AUTOLANDER_HANDBACK` structured stdout markers are named as 'structured' in S5c but their concrete format — field names, encoding (JSON object? key=value pairs? a bare token followed by JSON?), and the parsing key `observability.sh` must grep/match on — is never defined anywhere in the plan. The emitter (`loop.py`) and the parser (`observability.sh`) have no shared schema, making the feeder contract between them ambiguous.
findings.location: S5c AC2 (Error metric, three feeders) + S5c Files (observability.sh EDIT)

findings.finding: The new HTTP status endpoint stood up by S5c has no specified port, bind address, nginx proxy path, or human/admin authentication mechanism. The plan says 'bound so agents can reach it' and 'read-only, non-secret' but never states whether it is loopback-only, behind the existing nginx TLS gateway, or internet-reachable — and never provides a named auth mechanism or an explicit justified no-auth rationale for human/admin access.
findings.location: S5c (child ticket 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) AC1: 'Read-only status endpoint … bound so agents can reach it'; parent S5 AC: 'Observability + status endpoint'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5c AC1: bind the status endpoint on 127.0.0.1:<PORT> (loopback-only); add a `/autolander/` location to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` proxying to it if remote agent access is required; state explicitly 'no auth needed because the endpoint is behind the nginx TLS gateway and exposes only non-secret read-only data' as the no-auth rationale for human/admin access.

findings.finding: The heartbeat file path is not canonicalized across the children that write (S5d) and read (S5c) it. S5d says 'a timestamp file in the state volume' and S5c says 'heartbeat_age_s (integer seconds since the last heartbeat write)' — neither names the concrete file. By contrast, the lockfile ('autolander.lock') and recovery file ('recovery.json') are explicitly named. If S5d chooses one filename and S5c's endpoint reads a different path, the heartbeat_age_s field will silently always reflect a stale or missing file.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) AC 'Heartbeat (AC5)' + child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) AC 'Read-only status endpoint'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to S5d's heartbeat AC and S5c's status-endpoint AC: 'The heartbeat file is named heartbeat.ts at the root of the state volume (same root as autolander.lock and recovery.json).' Reference this name in S5a's state-volume AC for completeness.

findings.finding: The heartbeat file name/path is never contracted between S5d (which writes it) and S5c (which reads it for `heartbeat_age_s`) and S5d's HEALTHCHECK (which stat-s it). All three refer to 'a timestamp file in the state volume' or 'the heartbeat write' without specifying the concrete filename, creating an implicit shared state gap that could result in each component using a different path.
findings.location: child 38be-a778-43f5-4a4e (S5d) AC Heartbeat; child 9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03 (S5c) AC Read-only status endpoint
findings.suggested_fix: In S5d's Heartbeat AC, pin the filename: 'the bot writes `{mount}/heartbeat.txt` (a UTC ISO-8601 timestamp)'; in S5c's status endpoint AC add: 'reads `heartbeat_age_s` from `{mount}/heartbeat.txt`'; in S5d's HEALTHCHECK AC add: 'stats `{mount}/heartbeat.txt`'.

findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint (S5c) is network-reachable by agents but its access posture is never formally declared — the plan says 'Read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether it is public-with-rationale, internal-only, or requires any access control, nor does it name the bind address or network scope.
findings.location: S5c child ticket (9a6f-065d-91c4-4e03) — AC1 (Read-only status endpoint)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S5c AC1: 'The endpoint MUST bind to the host's loopback or internal Docker network interface only (not 0.0.0.0), is classified internal-only / unauthenticated, and is reachable only from agents co-located on the same host or private network.' If wider access is intended, state the rationale explicitly.

findings.finding: The read-only HTTP status endpoint introduced in S5c is a network-reachable surface (`land`'s `lander_down` reads it remotely; the AC says 'bound so agents can reach it') but its access posture is never explicitly declared — the plan says 'Read-only, non-secret' but does not state whether it is public-with-rationale, internal-network-only, or loopback-restricted. Under the rubric, an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the finding, regardless of whether the data is sensitive.
findings.location: S5c AC — 'Read-only status endpoint': 'bound so agents can reach it'; S5c child ticket description
findings.suggested_fix: In S5c's AC for the status endpoint, add: 'The endpoint MUST bind to 127.0.0.1 (or the Docker-internal network only) and NOT be published on a host port reachable from outside the Gerrit box, so no auth or TLS is required; if cross-host access is needed (e.g., land running on a different host), document TLS-or-SSH-tunnel requirement.' This makes the access posture explicit and auditable.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
